query: hypothesis that prolactin is solely under inhibitory control of hypothalamus

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Anterior Pituitary
retrieved_docs.content: Prolactin is a hormone produced by lactotrophs found in the anterior pituitary gland. Prolactin regulation is by the hypothalamus in an inhibitory manner – dopamine is released from the hypothalamus to decrease prolactin secretion. All other hormones depend on a stimulation signal from the hypothalamus to be synthesized and released. This explains why prolactin levels increase with the severing of the HPA axis, whereas other hormone levels decrease. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Gland -- Issues of Concern -- Prolactin [8]
retrieved_docs.content: Precursor cells: mainly from lactotrophs in the AP Target cells: main target cells are mammary glands and gonads Mechanism of action: binds to peptide hormone receptor (single transmembrane domain) to activate the JAK2-STAT intracellular signaling pathway similar to that of GH Regulation: Like GH, dual hypothalamic inhibitory (from dopamine) and stimulatory hormones (PRH) regulate prolactin secretion. The predominant hypothalamic influence is inhibitory.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prolactin -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) and dopamine are both released by the hypothalamus and have activity in modulating lactotophic activity. Dopamine is tonically (ie, continuous) secreted by dopaminergic neurons that project from the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus into the anterior pituitary gland via the infundibulum. This pathway is called the tuberoinfundibular pathway. The dopamine released at the terminal buttons of the nerves acts on lactotrophic cells through D2 receptors, modulating intracellular signaling and inhibiting prolactin synthesis. In the absence of pregnancy (ie, high estrogen) or lactation in sexually mature females, prolactin is constitutively inhibited by dopamine, and the effect of dopamine trumps the effect of minimal stimulatory effects of TRH. Additionally, prolactin has negative feedback on its own production by stimulating the release of dopamine in the hypothalamus. Medications that antagonize dopamine production (ie, antipsychotics) or masses that compress the infundibulum disrupt the basal inhibition of prolactin synthesis, resulting in hyperprolactinemia. Conversely, medications that are dopamine agonists, such as bromocriptine or cabergoline, will inhibit prolactin secretion and can be used to treat pathologies associated with hyperprolactinemia. Lastly, the decidua layer of the placenta has also been found to synthesize prolactin. This process is independent of dopamine regulation. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prolactin -- Clinical Significance -- Medication-induced Prolactinemia
retrieved_docs.content: First-generation antipsychotics such as haloperidol and fluphenazine can cause symptoms of prolactin excess, such as galactorrhea and gynecomastia, through their dopamine blockade. The specific mechanism involves non-selective D2 dopamine-receptor inhibiting in multiple dopaminergic pathways, including the tuberoinfundibular tract, where dopamine tonically inhibits prolactin, resulting in disinhibition of prolactin release. The loss of dopamine-mediated disinhibition allows for constitutively active lactotrophic cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Anterior Pituitary
retrieved_docs.content: In primary hypothyroidism, TRH elevates in an attempt to increase TSH levels. TRH can act on the anterior pituitary to increase prolactin levels. Anti-psychotics are dopamine antagonists, meaning the usual inhibitory hormone is lost – resulting in elevated prolactin. In either of the above scenarios, hyperprolactinemia is present in addition to several others (prolactinoma, for example). Elevated prolactin inhibits GnRH, and decreased pulsation leads to decreased levels of FSH and LH. Commonly exhibited symptoms include amenorrhea and infertility in both males and females. [35] [36] [37] [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Issues During Pregnancy -- Function -- Prolactin Axis
retrieved_docs.content: The increase in PRL secretion is important for the development of breast tissue for lactation. Prolactin is the only anterior pituitary hormone that does not have an endocrine target tissue, so it is not under the regulation of classical hormonal feedback. Instead, there is short loop feedback, where prolactin stimulates the secretion of its own inhibitor, dopamine. The dopamine neurons are present in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus. In pregnancy, the lactotroph cells' hyperplasia leads to the enlargement of the pituitary gland. Estrogen and progesterone both stimulate maternal PRL production. The majority of PRL derives from the maternal pituitary gland, while a contribution of the decidua and the fetal pituitary gland is small. Other factors which stimulate maternal pituitary PRL secretion, is thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), arginine, meals, and sleep similar to what we expect in nonpregnant women. [7] [3] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Adenohypophysis (Pars Anterior, Anterior Pituitary) -- Structure and Function -- Prolactin (PRL)
retrieved_docs.content: The hypothalamus primarily controls prolactin. Dopamine is the predominant hormone that inhibits the secretion of prolactin. When the hypothalamus receives afferent sensory nerve cell input from the nipple stimulation of lactating women, it causes the feedback loop of inhibition of prolactin to reverse. Due to the suckling, dopamine release is inhibited. This would allow an unopposed release of prolactin. Prolactin helps with the growth and development of the breasts and the maintenance of lactation in women. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prolactin -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of prolactin can include either lack of prolactin production or excessive prolactin production. Prolactin deficiency results in failure to lactate, while excessive prolactin results in galactorrhea and infertility. Destruction of the anterior pituitary can cause prolactin deficiency. On the other hand, prolactin excess causes include loss of dopamine inhibition under the influence of antipsychotic drugs or destruction of the hypothalamus/hypothalami-hypophyseal tract. Prolactin-secreting tumors can also cause prolactin excess, as is the case with prolactinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prolactin -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Anterior Pituitary
retrieved_docs.content: When prolactin is secreted, it stimulates milk production in the mammary glands. During pregnancy, elevated estrogen acts on the anterior pituitary to further increase prolactin secretion, preparing the mammary glands for breastfeeding. However, progesterone levels also become elevated, inhibiting prolactin at the breast. This is why milk secretion does not begin until after birth, because postpartum physiology results in drastically decreased progesterone levels, resulting in loss of prolactin inhibition. [33] It is discussed in detail here. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Gland -- Issues of Concern -- Prolactin [8]
retrieved_docs.content: Physiological Functions: The main functions of prolactin are stimulating mammary gland growth and development (mammographic effect) and milk production (lactogenic effect). It also has effects on the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and can inhibit pulsatile GnRH secretion from the hypothalamus.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Hormones -- Cellular Level -- Prolactin
retrieved_docs.content: Prolactin is a protein hormone secreted by the acidophilic lactotroph cells of the anterior pituitary gland. Chemically, prolactin is similar to a growth hormone composed of 199 amino acids and forms after a 28-amino acid signal peptide is proteolytically cleaved from the prolactin prohormone (pre-prolactin). [3] The secretion of prolactin by the anterior pituitary is tonically inhibited by dopamine from the tuberoinfundibular pathway of the hypothalamus and stimulated by thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), estrogen, dopamine antagonist (antipsychotics), and multiple factors, including suckling, stress, and sleep.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hypothalamus -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperprolactinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Prolactin release is inhibited by the release of dopamine from the hypothalamus. Therefore, any condition that decreases the release of dopamine leads to an uninhibited excess of prolactin secretion by the lactotroph cells of the pituitary gland. Some common causes of hyperprolactinemia include lactotroph adenomas, damage to the dopaminergic neurons of the hypothalamus, and dopaminergic antagonist medications such as antipsychotics. [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Hypothalamus
retrieved_docs.content: Dopamine is an important molecule in the human body; it plays roles in many organs. It is most commonly discussed in psychiatric and neurological settings due to its role as a neurotransmitter. However, dopamine is also an endocrine hormone secreted from the hypothalamus to the pituitary. The primary role of endocrine hormone physiology is to inhibit prolactin secretion. It has been well established and studied that pathology or medication side effects of decreased dopamine lead to hyperprolactinemia and the pathophysiology associated with this state. [26] It is discussed in detail in this article. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prolactin -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Regardless of the etiology of excess prolactin, infertility ensues in both sexes due to GnRH inhibition by prolactin, as well as galactorrhea or the inappropriate flow of breast milk in men and women. Dopamine agonist medications such as bromocriptine and cabergoline are treatment options for prolactin excess regardless of its etiology. It is important to know that hypothyroidism can present with galactorrhea. As mentioned before, TRH has a stimulatory effect on prolactin; thus, with low thyroid hormone in hypothyroidism, the negative feedback on TRH is removed, allowing for excessive prolactin release and galactorrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: Prolactinoma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hypothalamus has a predominant inhibitory influence on prolactin secretion via dopamine, and any factor which disrupts this mechanism causes hyperprolactinemia. It is important to consider the various causes of hyperprolactinemia, as increased prolactin secretion is noted in many physiological and pathological states other than prolactinomas.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1303
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES is defined as an overarching class of endocrine agents that includes both anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues as well as hypothalamic hormones (hypotalamické hormony). In this hierarchy, anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues are considered a subset of the broader PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES group, reflecting the close functional relationship between pituitary secretions and their regulatory hypothalamic counterparts. At the same time, the entire PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES category is regarded as a type of hypothalamic hormone group, underscoring the integrated nature of pituitary‑hypothalamic endocrine pathways and the inclusion of synthetic analogues that mimic these natural hormones. This comprehensive classification aids in understanding the shared physiology, therapeutic targeting, and clinical management of disorders involving pituitary and hypothalamic hormone systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1893
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin and its analogues are classified as posterior pituitary lobe hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2955
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists constitute a class of compounds that stimulate the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone receptor to modulate pituitary hormone release, and triptorelin is a specific agent that belongs to this LHRH‑agonist class. Recognizing triptorelin as a member of the LHRH‑agonist group is essential for understanding its mechanism of action and therapeutic applications in hormone‑dependent conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist is classified as a hormone and related agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2080
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is used to denote hormones that inhibit the release of pituitary hormones, and this inhibitory action is recognized as an effect of drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2003
retrieved_docs.content: Hypothalamic hormones include a subgroup referred to as hormony inhibující uvolňování hypofyzárního hormonu, which function to inhibit the release of pituitary hormones and thereby regulate endocrine activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 472
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin and analogues are classified as posterior pituitary lobe hormones and are members of the oxytocin family, indicating that these compounds are produced by the posterior pituitary and share the biological characteristics of oxytocin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1417
retrieved_docs.content: ACTH and synthetic analog preparations are classified as anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues, indicating that they belong to the group of hormones produced by the anterior pituitary gland as well as their synthetic counterparts. Cosyntropin is identified as a member of the ACTH and synthetic analog preparations group, representing a specific synthetic ACTH analogue used in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 621
retrieved_docs.content: ACTH and synthetic analog preparations are classified as anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues, and cosyntropin, a synthetic ACTH analog, is a member of this group. The broader class PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES includes anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues and is itself a subclass of hypotalamické hormony (hypothalamic hormones). This hierarchy places ACTH and its synthetic analogs within the larger framework of pituitary and hypothalamic hormonal systems, linking them to both anterior pituitary hormone activity and the broader category of hypothalamic hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: the relationships illustrate how anterior pituitary hormones and their synthetic analogues are organized within the larger pituitary‑hypothalamic hormonal framework, how therapeutic groups such as somatropin agonists and thyrotropin agents are defined, and how a diverse array of diagnostic agents—including hormone analogues, microbial preparations, and imaging compounds—are integral to assessing and managing endocrine function and related pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: posterior pituitary lobe hormones of the oxytocin family, have a defined relationship with the uterus, reflecting their role in stimulating uterine contractions during labor and in assisted delivery procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin and its analogues are classified as posterior pituitary lobe hormones and are members of the oxytocin family. Vasopressin and its analogues are likewise posterior pituitary lobe hormones, with desmopressin and terlipresin identified as specific members of the vasopressin analogue group. Desmopressin is contraindicated in patients who have اضطراب الكلية (kidney disorder). Recognizing that both oxytocin‑related and vasopressin‑related compounds originate from the posterior pituitary informs clinical decisions about hormone replacement and the safe use of synthetic analogues, particularly avoiding desmopressin in individuals with renal impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES is positioned within the broader group of hypotalamické hormony (hypothalamic hormones) and serves as an overarching category that includes anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues. Both Somatropin and somatropin agonists and Thyrotropin Class in ATC are classified as subclasses of anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues. Within the Somatropin and somatropin agonists subclass, the specific therapeutic agents mecasermin, somapacitan and tesamorelin are recognized as members, reflecting their shared mechanism of action as growth‑hormone related compounds. Similarly, the Thyrotropin Class in ATC includes thyreotropin alfa as a member, representing a specific thyroid‑stimulating hormone analogue. This hierarchical organization links broad endocrine categories to individual hormone analogues and agents, facilitating clear pharmacological classification and supporting informed clinical decision‑making in endocrine therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1124
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin and its analogues are classified as hormones of the posterior pituitary lobe, where they play key roles in water retention and vascular tone regulation. Within this group, terlipresin is recognized as a specific synthetic analogue, making it a member of the broader vasopressin‑analogue class. Understanding that vasopressin and its analogues, including terlipresin, originate from posterior pituitary physiology clarifies their mechanisms of action and informs their therapeutic application in conditions such as hypotension, variceal bleeding, and certain types of shock.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770
retrieved_docs.content: Dopamine is classified as a sympathomimetic, meaning it mimics the actions of the sympathetic nervous system.
